content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance · · Administration · · Executive/Legal · · Enforcement · · Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662         322-5660          322-5901         322-6441         322-6444

May 26, 1981


Mari Kitsuse, Esq.
Beckman & Beaudikofer
Suite 23, Sandy Plaza Bldg.
599 N. Mathilda Avenue
Sunnyvale, CA  94086

Dear Ms. Kitsuse:

     Thank you for your letter of May 13, 1981, requesting
advice from this office.  Since you requested advice in hypo-
thetical form, the immunity contained in Government Code Section
83114(b)1/ cannot be granted.

     You posed the facts as follows.  An elected official
whose position is specified in Section 87200 establishes Corporation
A in which he is the sole shareholder.  Corporation A purchases
40% of the stock in Corporation B.  Both corporations do business
in the official's jurisdiction.  Your questions concerned the
extent of the official's reporting obligations in this situation.

     Persons whose positions are listed in Section 87200 of
the Political Reform Act are required to file annual statements
disclosing their investments, interests in real property and
income during the period covered by the statement.  Section
87203.

     The definition of "investment" includes "a pro rata share
of investments of any business entity . . . in which the individual
or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, -
a 10 percent interest or greater."  Section 82034.  Since the
official in the hypothetical situation has a 10 percent or
greater interest, i.e., 100% in Corporation A, he must report
his pro rata share, i.e., 100% of the investments of Corporation A.
This would include, of course, the investment in Corporation B.

_______________________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code.

Mari Kitsuse, Esq.
May 26, 1981
Page Two


     Correspondingly, the definition of "income' includes "a
pro rata share of any income of any business entity ... in
which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater." Section
82030. Under the hypothetical facts, the official would have
to report his pro rata share, i.e., 100%, of the income Corpora-
tion A receives from Corporation B.

     I thust this discussion has been helpful. if I can be
further assistance, please feel free to contact me at (916)
322-5901.

                                          Very truly yours,

                                          [signature]
                                          Diane Maura Fishburn
                                          Counsel
                                          Legal Division

DMF:plh

LAW OFFICES OF
BECKMAN & BEAUDIKOFER
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
(408) 736-4747
(408) 733-6111

ERIC BEAUDIKOFER
JOHN R. BECKMAN
MARI KITSUSE

SUITE 23, SANDY PLAZA BLDG.
599 N. MATHILDA AVE.
SUNNYVALE, CALIF. 94086

OF COUNSEL
RICHARD L. SPECTOR

May 13, 1981

State of California
Fair Political Practices
Commission
1100 K Street, 2nd Floor
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Sir or Madam:

     This letter is in regards to the required
disclosures of economic interests under Proposition 9.
I would like an opinion letter from your office in
response to the following hypothetical:

     Assume that an elected official within Gov.
Code §87200 establishes a corporation in which he is
the sole shareholder (Corporation "A"). Corporation
A then purchases 40% of the stock in Corporation "B".
Both corporations do business in the official's
jurisdiction.

     (1) Will the official be required to disclose
         Corporation A's purchase of stock in
         Corporation B?

     (2) Will the official be required to disclose
         Corporation A's income received from
         Corporation B?

     I would appreciate a reply at your earliest
convenience. Thank you for your assistance.

                                   Very truly yours,

                                   LAW OFFICES OF
                                   BECKMAN & BEAUDIKOFER


                                   BY: ___________________
                                       MARI KITSUSE

MK/clt

Since you requested advice in hypothetical form, the immunity contained in Government Code Section 83114(b) cannot be granted. You posed the facts as follows. An elected official whose position is specified in Section 87200 establishes Corporation A in which he is the sole shareholder. Corporation A purchases 40% of the stock in Corporation B. Both corporations do business in the official's jurisdiction. Your questions concerned the extent of the official's reporting obligations in this situation. Persons whose positions are listed in Section 87200 of the Political Reform Act are required to file annual statements disclosing their investments, interests in real property and income during the period covered by the statement. Section 87203. The definition of "investment" includes "a pro rata share of investments of any business entity ... in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater." Section 82034. Since the official in the hypothetical situation has a 10 percent or greater interest, i.e., 100% in Corporation A, he must report his pro rata share, i.e., 100% of the investments of Corporation A. This would include, of course, the investment in Corporation B. 1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code. Correspondingly, the definition of "income" includes "a pro rata share of any income of any business entity ... in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10 percent interest or greater." Section 82030. Under the hypothetical facts, the official would have to report his pro rata share, i.e., 100%, of the income Corporation A receives from Corporation B. I trust this discussion has been helpful. If I can be further assistance, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.
